notes: xDrive has been the standard AWD suffix since 2003. Older models (notably E46) used lowercase 'xi' for petrol AWD saloons/touring and 'xd' for diesel AWD (e.g. 325xi, 530xd). SUV X models can be xDrive or purely electric variants; absence of xDrive on a non-X model usually implies RWD.

Audi markets AWD with the trademark 'quattro' (lowercase in official European badging and model strings). Many Q-badged SUVs are quattro by default but the word still appears in official variant names. S and RS models often include quattro in the full designation.
4MATIC is the standard Mercedes-Benz trademark for all-wheel drive and appears in official European model and variant names. EQ electric models use the same 4MATIC suffix for dual-motor AWD versions.

4MOTION is VW's AWD brand for combustion and some ICE-based hybrids. On ID. family EVs, dual-motor AWD performance variants are branded GTX in Europe (US may use different naming). Pre-1998 4WD VWs were often sold under the Syncro name.

On 911 and similar lines, AWD is indicated by a '4' in the model name (Carrera 4, 4S, Targa 4, etc.)—match as part of the variant string, not a separate word. Modern 911 Turbo / Turbo S are all-wheel drive despite no '4' in the name. Many other Porsche models (Cayenne, Macan, Taycan 4S/ Turbo) use trim names and numerals; Taycan cross/utility trims often denote AWD via dual-motor grade names rather than one fixed keyword.
ALL4 denotes MINI's all-wheel-drive system on Countryman and historically Clubman. Many MINI hatch models remain FWD only; absence of ALL4 implies FWD.

Stellantis-era plug-in AWD crossovers use Hybrid4 (rear e-motor). Older Insignia and other models were sometimes badged or listed with '4x4'. Twinster is the rear-axle torque-vectoring technology name and may not always appear in the public model title; verify from specs when absent.

Volvo typically appends 'AWD' to the variant name when all-wheel drive is fitted. Pure electric models may use dual-motor grades without the letters 'AWD' in every market string—fallback to technical data may be needed.

Saab ended passenger car production under the original company circa 2012. Late 9-3 AWD used the XWD branding (Haldex Gen 4–based). Earlier Saab AWD models may appear as 'AWD' in listings. Many classic Saabs were FWD only.

examples: Polestar 2 Long range Dual motor with Performance pack
notes: European Polestar 2 configurations use 'Dual Motor' phrasing for AWD. Single-motor variants are FWD or RWD depending on generation—no shared keyword for non-AWD.

Most Peugeots in Europe are FWD. Plug-in AWD uses HYBRID4 (rear electric axle). Some older or special versions used '4x4' in naming. If no such keyword appears, assume FWD unless build data says otherwise.

Mainstream Citroën C4/C5 Aircross hybrid and PHEV lines in Europe are predominantly FWD in recent generations; AWD PHEV variants, when sold, may not use a single consistent public keyword across all markets. Commercial/rare models historically used '4x4'. For reliable AWD branding with '4x4' in the name, see DS Automobiles (E-Tense 4x4).

examples: Austral E-Tech full hybrid (FWD—no AWD keyword)
notes: Many current Renault passenger models in Europe are FWD; all-wheel-drive options are limited and often not distinguished by a dedicated trademark in the model name. Older SUVs or special editions sometimes used '4x4'. 4CONTROL is rear-wheel steering on some models, not AWD—do not treat it as an AWD keyword.
Alpine A110 range sold in Europe is rear-wheel drive; no AWD keyword applies.
Plug-in hybrid AWD models add '4x4' (often as 'E-Tense 4x4') to the official name. Other trims may be FWD only.
Q4 is Alfa Romeo's all-wheel-drive system branding on Giulia, Stelvio, Tonale, and related lines in European materials. Base versions without Q4 are often RWD or FWD depending on model.
Maserati uses Q4 for intelligent AWD on SUVs and some passenger cars in European communications. UK and other listings sometimes abbreviate to 'AWD' in derivative text—both indicate all-wheel drive.
AWD is optional on selected 500X and related Stellantis small SUVs and appears as '4x4' in official variant names. Panda 4x4 is a long-standing AWD city car name. Most Fiat models remain FWD.

UK/European Toyota hybrid AWD often uses 'AWD-i' (electric rear axle). Some models use plain 'AWD' or 'E-Four' depending on powertrain and market brochure. Commercial Hilux/Land Cruiser variants may use '4x4' or grade names instead—context matters.

Hybrid AWD is frequently branded E-FOUR / E-Four in Europe. Battery EV RZ uses DIRECT4 torque control on dual-motor versions; check generation-specific naming. ICE or mixed listings may say 'AWD' explicitly.
Mazda markets mechanical AWD as i-Activ AWD in brochures; configurators may shorten to 'AWD' at the end of the derivative. MX-5 and many Mazda2/Mazda3 trims are FWD/RWD only.
European Honda SUVs with all-wheel drive typically append 'AWD' after the powertrain label (e.g. e:HEV AWD). Civic and smaller models sold in Europe are generally FWD.

e-4ORCE is Nissan's dual-motor EV/e-POWER AWD brand on X-Trail, Ariya, and related lines. Traditional mechanical 4WD may appear as '4WD' in the variant string. Older SUVs sometimes used '4x4' in naming.

Subaru passenger vehicles sold in Europe use Symmetrical AWD as standard with no separate AWD keyword in the model name. (Historically some non-European base Impreza/Legacy were FWD; European lineups are effectively all AWD.)

Super-All Wheel Control (S-AWC) is the technology name but is not consistently part of the customer-facing model title. Current Outlander PHEV for Europe is twin-motor AWD across trims—treat as AWD by model line. Other models may be 2WD or 4WD; listings sometimes include '4WD' or '4x4' in the derivative.
ALLGRIP is Suzuki's European branding for selectable or permanent AWD on Vitara, S-Cross, Swace, etc. Base trims without ALLGRIP are FWD. Older Jimny/SX4 sometimes used '4x4' in naming.

Hyundai uses the HTRAC technology brand globally, but European price lists and press materials often show '4WD' or 'AWD' at the end of the derivative rather than the word HTRAC. Match both where present. Ioniq 5/6 AWD is usually indicated via dual-motor grade names (e.g. AWD in spec tables).

Kia generally appends 'AWD' or '4WD' to the official variant description when all-wheel drive is fitted; there is no separate single trademark like quattro. EV models use motor layout in the trim name.

Genesis Europe lists drivetrain as 'AWD' in configuration names. HTRAC is the underlying Hyundai Motor Group AWD system name and may appear in technical articles but not always in the public model title.
Jaguar uses 'AWD' in official derivative names when all-wheel drive is specified. Many base trims are RWD; absence of AWD implies RWD.

All Land Rover and Range Rover passenger vehicles sold in Europe are engineered as permanent four-wheel-drive / all-wheel-drive vehicles; there is no separate AWD keyword in the model name. Older Series models were part-time 4WD—still no modern-style name suffix.

Modern Bentley cars in European markets (Continental, Flying Spur, Bentayga, etc.) use permanent all-wheel drive without a dedicated AWD word in the model name. Very old pre-2000s models were often RWD—use vehicle year and specs for those.

Cullinan and Spectre are dual-motor or AWD architectures without an AWD keyword in the name. Phantom, Ghost, and Wraith/Dawn generations are predominantly rear-wheel drive. No single keyword covers the brand—manual confirmation is safest.

Škoda uses '4x4' in official European naming for combustion AWD (VW Group convention differs from VW's 4MOTION label). Enyaq dual-motor variants often use an 'x' suffix (e.g. 80x) rather than the word 4x4—treat x-suffix trims as AWD in EV context.

SEAT historically used 4Drive on models such as Tarraco and some Leon/Ateca configurations. SEAT passenger range is winding down in favour of Cupra; used listings remain relevant for the car-journal era.
Cupra uses 4Drive for Haldex-type AWD on Formentor, Ateca, and related performance variants. Some e-Hybrid models use rear-motor AWD without 4Drive in the name—check powertrain.

Marketing copy uses 'Intelligent AWD' / 'Intelligent All-Wheel Drive' for disconnect-type systems; configurators often end with 'AWD'. Ranger and commercial 4x4 pick-ups may use '4x4' or Wildtrak etc.—trim-led naming.
European Tesla designations use 'Long Range AWD', 'Dual Motor', or 'Performance' (dual motor) to denote all-wheel drive. Rear-wheel-drive-only trims omit these phrases.

Plug-in hybrids use the 4xe suffix. Many combustion Jeeps are inherently 4WD-focused but trims vary (e.g. some small SUVs offer FWD in Europe). 'Trailhawk' signals off-road trim, not strictly AWD by itself. Verify per variant.
BYD Europe uses 'AWD' or 'Excellence AWD' / 'Performance' on dual-motor EVs depending on model line. DM-i AWD PHEVs may include 'AWD' in the grade name.

examples: MG ZS EV Long Range (FWD example)
notes: MG4 XPower is the clear dual-motor AWD performance badge. Dealer listings sometimes append '4WD' and '(Dual Motor)' in the derivative string for AWD EVs. Most MG trims remain FWD.

NIO European materials describe AWD via dual-motor powertrains; exact spelling ('Dual Motor' vs localised copy) can vary by market site. Single-motor variants are not AWD—check motor count in specifications.
